chapters.title: Adults' learning advantage
chapters.text:
Imagine yourself sitting in a small restaurant in Rome, discussing the menu with a server in Italian, and reading a local newspaper while waiting for your order. Many of us dream about fluency in foreign languages that will bring this scenario to life. Yet, as soon as we open language self-study books, thoughts like "You should have an inborn talent to reach fluency" or "The older you get, the harder mastering languages becomes" pop up.

These doubts are baseless. Everyone can reach fluency in any language. Moreover, although adults can't surpass children in terms of free time for studying, they notice patterns quickly. And since language is all about patterns, adults actually learn faster than children.

The trick is having motivation, commitment, and the right learning plan. Take Gabriel Wyner as an example. After years of studying Hebrew at school, he barely remembers the alphabet. Yet, as soon as he decided to become an opera singer, he approached foreign languages more seriously. Instead of randomly trying one technique after another, he has analyzed how memory works and used it for his goal.

Only you can make a foreign language truly yours.

Join us on this learning journey to refine your pronunciation, recall vocabulary easily, and personalize your learning to reach fluency faster. Plus, in the end, you'll get five effective strategies to boost your vocabulary.

chapters.title: Connections decide everything
chapters.text:
Let's start with the elephant in the room: how can you place so many foreign words, grammar rules, and phrases into long-term memory? The principles of how the brain works provide some hints.

It's common to depict memory as a massive amount of information gathered in one place. However, the truth is more nuanced. Memories rely on many brain parts and especially on connections between neurons. As Hebb's Law states, neurons that fire together wire together. Thus, the more connections memories have, the more lasting they become.

To check it out, let's play a quick game. Please answer these four questions:

• How many letters does the word HARE have?
• Do BIRD and HERD rhyme?
• Are CUP and MUG synonyms?
• Do you like ICE CREAM?

Now, can you recall the words from the four questions we just asked you? Do you remember all of them? This game resembles a test that psychologists created in the 1970s. They made each question appeal to a certain level of processing information: structure, sound, concept, and personal connection. When scientists compared words participants recalled, the absolute champion was one related to a personal connection (in our case — ice cream).

Why? Personal connections evoke dozens of other memories; thus, the brain treats them as the most important. The top-recalled words in order after personal connection were concept-related (like cup and mug), sound-related (such as bird and herd), and, lastly, structure-related (like hare). In a nutshell, the fewer connections the word required, the faster people forgot it.

The consequences of this principle for learning languages are paramount. If you want new words and phrases to stick in your mind, activate all four levels of processing:

• Understand the word's structure: What letters does it have?
• Hear how it sounds: Are there any patterns between structure and sound?
• Determine what concept it refers to: What does it mean? How does it relate to words you already know? Images are indispensable at this level as human visual memory is incredible. In one experiment, after participants viewed 612 magazine ads, scientists mixed these images with new ones and presented them again. Participants indicated ads they had previously seen in almost 99% of the cases.
• Establish personal connections: For instance, ask yourself, "When did I try helado (ice cream in Spanish) for the first time?" or "Am I exigente (a Spanish word for demanding)?"

Strive to grasp language as a network, not a set of fragments.

chapters.title: The recipe for effective revising
chapters.text:
The four levels of processing help you remember words and grammar rules, but not for long. According to the "forgetting curve," discovered by psychologist Hermann Ebbinghaus in 1885, we forget the fastest immediately after learning. For instance, in an hour, we remember around 50% of the studied information; in 24 hours, we recall around 30%. Then, the speed slows down, and we recollect at least 25% in a week.

How can you remember longer? Ebbinghaus noticed that the revision of information reduces forgetting. However, as modern science proves, revisions should match specific requirements.

1. Ensure you recall, not read
In one experiment, participants received money for each foreign word they recalled. They had two options:

• Read a list of foreign words twice.
• Read the list once, write down words they recall, and give the paper to scientists.

Then, the researchers assessed how much the participants remembered after various periods. In five minutes, those who read twice (let's call them Group A) recalled 80%, while those who read and wrote the words down (let's call them Group B) recollected 75%. Tested two days later, Group A mentioned 55% of those words, and Group B named almost 70%. In a week, Group B surpassed Group A by 35%.

Why? Group A practiced reading the words, while Group B focused on recalling. Besides, writing something on paper makes you feel judged by others or yourself, improving your performance.

2. Make recalling challenging enough
Revising well-known words gets boring very quickly. To avoid this, use an advanced version of flashcards — spaced repetition systems (SRS). Their principle is simple: the better you recall a word, the less you'll repeat it. As a result, you'll learn 15-30 new words daily and revise the ones you're about to forget.

Always aim to deepen your understanding — a few extra seconds of reflection can turn knowledge into mastery.

3. Don't use others' SRS
Whether you create your SRS by hand or use a computer-based one, like Anki, flashcards should reflect four levels of your processing of some words. For instance, you can relate ratón (mouse in Spanish) with a cartoon Tom and Jerry. Adding the image of Jerry to the back side of your flashcard will create more connections and significantly increase your chances of remembering the word.

When you create something, it becomes a part of you. ~ Gabriel Wyner

Excited to supercharge your language skills? Practical strategies are waiting for you in the "Try this" section.

chapters.title: How to sound like a native
chapters.text:
Now that you know how to remember, let's delve into what you should focus on. We'll start with correct pronunciation, one of the most overlooked language aspects. Without it, you won't decipher what others are discussing, and native speakers will hardly understand you. The route toward correct pronunciation rests on three techniques.

1. Ear training
We were all born as masters of pronunciation, capable of recognizing phonemes from any language. But as we grew, we noticed some sounds distinguish words in our native language, like lock and rock, while others weren't used. By one year old, we had enough statistics to determine the sounds we should pay attention to and so, we forgot the rest. Thus, we can't immediately recognize and repeat when we hear foreign words with phonemes our native language lacks.

What should you do, then? Teaching your brain to identify new sounds is critical, and minimal pairs are great tools to do it. They comprise words that differ by one phoneme, like rice and rise or price and prize. You can find them in any pronunciation or grammar book, not to mention websites. After listening to each pair and checking yourself, you'll quickly grasp new sounds. Stanford and Carnegie Mellon researchers proved that three 20-minute sessions with minimal pairs for one sound are enough.

2. Mouth training
If the phoneme significantly differs from your native language, investigate how the sound is formed. It's not rocket science and comprises only three elements: positions of tongue, lips, and vocal cords.

Rely on resources with IPA (International Phonetic Alphabet) spelling, not "Englishy" explanations, like bawn-JURE.

3. Eye training
Learning pronunciation gets much easier when you know all the phonemes in a chosen foreign language. Besides, you don't need IPA spelling for each new word. Instead, it's time to apply an adult's superpower — noticing patterns. For instance, in words such as apples, dogs, and leaves, "s" is pronounced as [z], while in ships, pets, and bricks, it sounds like [s]. Grouping words by patterns helps you remember them easier, while SRS makes these memories long-lasting.

Did you know? An old singer’s trick can help you with complex words. Go backward: start with the ending and add one or two sounds until you pronounce the whole word automatically.

chapters.title: Turn abstract into specific
chapters.text:
Indeed, the number of foreign words and grammar rules may seem daunting. Yet, even this amount of data is manageable with spaced repetition systems. For instance, just 30 minutes daily results in 2,000-3,000 learned words in four months.

Although grammar has many rules and exceptions, you can summarize it in three main transformations:
• Adding words
• Changing word forms
• Changing word order

Train each transformation with SRS, writing sentences with gaps or creating memorable examples for each word form. Additionally, the following tricks will make learning even more effective.

1. Use Google Images as an inspiration source
When you don't have vivid associations with some foreign words, just google them. Looking through dozens of pictures, you'll find ones that surprise or shock you. Emotions are powerful, multi-sensory experiences — that's precisely what you need! Add these images to your flashcards.

2. Conquer grammatical gender with mnemonics
How can you remember that avocado in Spanish has a masculine gender and orange is feminine? New challenges require new tools, and that's when mnemonics comes to your rescue. Its main aim is to turn something abstract into vivid; for instance, let's connect masculine grammatical gender with dancing.

You can choose any action you like; the main thing is to make the word image memorable. Now, each time you encounter a word of masculine gender, such as avocado, book, and tea in Spanish, imagine them dancing.

The key to reaching fluency is adapting each learning activity to your preferences.

3. Use the person-action-object system for verb forms
If a foreign language has conjugation, apply these three steps:
• Identify patterns: For example, verb forms of fly, draw, and know follow a similar model, such as fly-flew-flown.
• Learn forms of one verb: Create sentences for each form of the verb fly. To make them more memorable, choose examples that tell different stories, be it about the flying machine of Leonardo da Vinci, your recent fight, or your favorite bird.
• Use mnemonics: Imagine a person (real or fictional) who does all the actions denoted by verbs with the same pattern. For instance, picture Marvel superhero Doctor Strange, who flies, knows secrets of time and draws magical circles. This mnemonic method is called the person-action-object system. Using it, you link verbs draw and know to the conjugation of fly.

chapters.title: A journey of a lifetime
chapters.text:
Learning foreign languages is always a personal expedition. It starts at the same point — basics of pronunciation, vocabulary, and grammar, but then unfolds depending on your destination. Along the way, you find tools that work best for you, engaging sources, and your community. The following pointers will guide your way:

• Be the architect of your vocabulary: If you want to travel, learn the 1000 most frequent words first.
• Revise flashcards regularly: SRS is crucial for recollecting all you've learned. If you missed some days, just revise everything you should and postpone learning new words until you catch up with the schedule.
• Take baby steps: For your first book in a foreign language, choose one you have already read in translation. It will make reading easier and help you focus on foreign words.
• Listen to audiobooks to enhance comprehension skills: You can make this process even more effective by reading the book while listening to it.
• Start with series, not films: After understanding the plot in the first episode, you'll feel more confident while watching the next ones. Yet, be wary of comedy series: it's frustrating to miss the punch line and have no clue why others are laughing.
• Use online resources for speaking: Some services quickly contact you with a native speaker who wants to learn your mother tongue. By contrast, others allow you to choose your language exchange partner. If you are unsure what to discuss, look for ideas on websites like ConversationStarters.com or YouRather.com.
• Write on topics you like (self-directed writing): You can boost its benefits by sending your texts to certain apps, like HelloTalk or HiNative, where others will edit them.

If you are learning a language for a particular purpose, consider specializing — consult thematic dictionaries.

All mentioned tools will make your learning journey easier and more fun as you approach fluency. Indeed, it won't be fast, but it will undoubtedly be worth it. Apart from the ability to speak with natives, language learning provides countless advantages, from salary rises to lower risks of dementia and Alzheimer's disease. You'll also benefit from the bilingual effect — better focus, multitasking, creativity, and problem-solving skills.

If you talk to a man in a language he understands, that goes to his head. If you talk to him in his language, that goes to his heart. ~ Nelson Mandela

chapters.title: Try this
chapters.text:
Mastering a new language doesn't have to be overwhelming. These practical steps will help you build fluency faster and make your learning stick:

• Create absurd examples: When learning adjectives or verbs, make bizarre comparisons. Instead of memorizing fast as rápido in Spanish, tell yourself, "A cheetah on roller skates is rápido!" Your brain loves the unexpected.
• Avoid translations: Instead of memorizing word lists in your native language, connect new words directly to images or experiences.
• Play a "forbidden words" challenge: Pick 3–5 basic words you use all the time (like very, good, or big) and ban them. Force yourself to find creative synonyms — this will skyrocket your vocabulary depth.
• Text yourself in a foreign language: Throughout the day, send yourself quick messages describing what you're doing, how you feel, or something funny that happened. Fluency grows through regular practice.
• Use "karaoke immersion": Find songs with lyrics in your target language and sing along, mimicking pronunciation as closely as possible. Bonus points if you act it out like a dramatic music video star.

Fluency isn't just about study — it's about playing, experimenting, and living in your new language. The weirder and more fun your methods, the faster you'll learn. So go ahead — get creative, and watch your skills take off!
